course_metadata.meeting_times: TUE and THU 11:10 am - 2:10 pm (Lecture and Lab)

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Development of system integration skills for solving real-world problems in computer science;
significant team software project that uses integration of advanced concepts across computer
science specializations; projects require design, implementation, documentation and
demonstration, as well as design methodology, management process and teamwork.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Senior classification; Grade of C or better in CSCE 411 and in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
C (communication-intensive course)

As a communication-intensive course, you will receive instruction on writing and public speaking and
complete assignments so you can practice communication related to the major. You must pass the
written and oral components with a C or better to earn passing grade in the course.

Failure to pass the communication components with a C or better results in an F for the course.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
Analyze a complex computing problem and apply principles of computing and other relevant disciplines to
identify solutions.

Design, implement, and evaluate a computing-based solution to meet a given set of computing
requirements in the context of the program's discipline. Communicate effectively in a variety of
professional contexts.
Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgments in computing practice based on
legal and ethical principles.
Collaborate effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in activities appropriate to the program's
discipline.
Apply computer science theory and software implementation fundamentals to produce computing-based
solutions.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Recommended:
•
Universal Principles of Design
•
How to Design Programs
•
Principles of Computer System Design
•
The 2020 Scrum Guide™м
•
Manifesto for Agile Software Development
•
Impact Engineering: Transforming Beyond Agile Project Management
•
Technical material from the literature, manufacturers' data sheets, and user manuals.

chunks.title: ABET Accreditation Information
chunks.content:
This course is evaluated every year through the ABET (Accreditation Board for Engineering and
Technology) accreditation process. ABET accreditation provides assurance that a college or university
program meets the quality standards of the profession for which that program prepares graduates. Your
degree is a significant achievement and perhaps the largest investment you will make toward your
future. The quality of education you receive makes a big difference in your career success.

ABET accreditation:
•
Verifies that your educational experience meets the global standard for technical education in
your profession.
•
Enhances your employment opportunities - many multinational organizations require
graduation from an accredited program.
•
Supports your entry to a technical profession through licensure, registration and certification all
of which often require graduation from an ABET-accredited program as a minimum qualification.
•
Establishes your eligibility for many federal student loans, grants, and/or scholarships.
•
Paves the way for you to work globally - ABET accreditation is recognized worldwide through
international agreements, and many other countries' national accrediting systems are based on
the ABET model.

In order for the department of Computer Science & Engineering to maintain our ABET accreditation, our
students must demonstrate their abilities in each of the above expected outcomes of this class. Each of
those skills will be evaluated by external industrial affiliate members. To complete the course, students
are required to participate in an exit interview with industry practitioners. If the course section is
selected to be reviewed as part of the capstone project review, the section's students are excused from
participating in the exit interviews.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The final grade you will receive in the class will be based on points accumulated during the semester.
Thus, both continued progress (the journey) and the quality of your product and other deliverables (the
destination) will determine your grade. Part of your grade is based on the performance of your team.
The other part of your grade is based on your individual performance (demonstration of mastery of
course objectives).
As a communication course, you must pass the communication components (both written and oral) with
a C or better to earn a passing grade in the course. Failure to pass the communication components with
a C or better results in an F for the course.
Any academic misconduct in this course will result in a grade of F* (the star (*) indicates academic
dishonesty).
If warranted, the course instructor, with input from the teaching assistant and the project sponsor, may
assign a failing grade regardless of a student's computed final score.
Failing to complete the User Study activity (part of the testing component) will result in a failing grade
in the course.
A score of less than 70% in any component (see "Grading Components" below) will result in a letter
grade reduction. This is how we implement mastery grading in this course. You are expected to revise
and resubmit work that falls below this threshold so that, in the end, you will have a score of at least 70%
in each component.
Grading Scale:
Grades will be assigned according to:
(define (grade x)
(cond ((< x 60) "F")
((< x 70) "D")
((< x 80) "C")
((< x 90) "B")
(else "A")))
These grades represent varying degrees of achievement (see the university's grading system): A =
excellent, B = good, C = satisfactory, D = passing, F = failing.

We do not curve in this course:
is not an A.
This course employs mastery grading which means that we have clear expectations in terms of the
knowledge and skills you must demonstrate by the end of the course to earn a C (a grade of D may be
"passing" for the University, but it is not "passing" for Computer Science and Engineering). If you
participate fully, revise and resubmit work based on feedback, and work equitably with your team, you
should easily earn at least a C. You will have opportunities to earn a grade higher than C by
demonstrating excellence above and beyond the minimum expectations of the course. How to earn
a B or A:
1. show us what you got: if you need to be told what to do for a grade above C, then you have
already failed to meet the expectations for a grade above C.
2. make us proud: if the instructor, department, or college would be embarrassed that you got a
grade above C, then you did not earn a grade above C.

Statistics:
| Grade | Count | % |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1455 | 84.94% |
| B | 200 | 11.68% |
| C | 47 | 2.74% |
| D | 6 | 0.35% |
| F | 5 | 0.29% |
Data: Grade Distribution, GPA Distribution, and Cumulative GPA Reports, 2019A - 2024A, CSCE 482.

Grading Components:
Your grade for this course reflects your mastery of course material and is determined by multiple
components. Note that you will not receive a passing grade in the class if you do not have a working,
tested, and evaluated system by the end of the semester. The lower weighted items will significantly
help you succeed in your higher-weighted items. Even if a component has a very low point value, it is
crucial to your later success in the higher point components.

| Component | Weight |
|---|---|
| Written Communication Elements | 20% |
| Project Completeness and Final Deliverables | 20% |
| Individual Contribution | 20% |
| Oral Communication Elements | 15% |
| Project Management | 5% |
| Testing | 5% |
| Status Updates | 5% |
| Ethics | 5% |
| Peer Feedback and Peer Evaluation | 3% |
| Recall Practices | 2% |
| Total | 100% |

Written Communication Elements - 20%: You will have several technical writing assignments including
(but not limited to) problem formation, related literature review, project proposals, documentation,
reflection, and your final report. Your writing will be critiqued on its thoroughness and effectiveness in
communicating key aspects of your system such as its motivation, approach, evaluation, and engineering
standard. Your documentation will be critiqued on its completeness to properly execute and understand
the details of your system. Some assignments will require you to incorporate peer feedback to refine
your communication.

Oral Communication Elements - 15%: You will have several presentation assignments. You will have
opportunity to practice presenting posters, more formal slide presentations, leading meetings, facilitating
discussions, and creating short videos. Some assignments will require you to incorporate peer feedback
to refine your communication. Your final presentation will be to the instructors and external panelists. All
team members must actively participate in the final presentation. You will also give live system
demonstrations to the class at multiple points in the course. Additionally, at least one member of your
team will present your project at the CSCE Capstone Expo, the college's Engineering Project Showcase,
and possibly other events to provide you with extra practice in communicating your work and responding
to external feedback.

Project Completeness and Final Deliverables - 20%: Your team will be assessed on the projects
completeness and scope through live demonstrations. You will also submit fully working, evaluated, and
documented code to your team's GitHub repository. All final deliverables are due on the last day of
class at 11:59pm. Course grades will not be assigned until all final deliverables have been turned in, all
borrowed items have been returned to their proper location or their owner including keys, books,
hardware, equipment, or other items supplied by the department, and the workstations in the lab have
been thoroughly cleaned up.

Individual Contribution - 20%: You will be assessed on your personal contribution to the team's success
with the project. This will be determined from status update evidence, team communication channels,
logged code commits, peer evaluations, engagement in team activities, and instructor/TA observations.
You are responsible for ensuring that your contributions are part of the record of the team's work on
the project, e.g. commits, pull requests, project tracker activity, notes from meetings, etc.

Project Management - 5%: You will create project management artifacts specific to your chosen software
development methodology. You will be also assessed on your team's ability to keep to the project
schedule. All teams will specify team member roles and produce user stories. You are also expected to
use GitHub for version control and commit to your team's repository multiple times a week during the
project development phase, if not daily. You are expected to use an industry-standard tool fro project
management/tracking, e.g. GitHub Projects, PivotalTracker, Trello, etc.

Testing - 5%: Your project must be evaluated in accordance with industry standards, so you need to test
the technical performance of your developed materials as your project goes on. You are expected to
develop an automated test suite for your project. You should also have appropriate unit testing,
integration testing, and system testing throughout your code to verify functionality. You will also have
user acceptance testing through a user study to evaluate your system's ability to solve your problem. All
team members must complete the required CITI Training before the team can begin a user study. You
will be assessed on how well you have prepared and conducted the evaluation of your system, both
internally (through unit/integration/system testing) and externally (through user study). Note that
completing the user study is a required part of the course and is not optional and you will, therefore,
receive a failing grade if you do not complete it.

Status Updates - 5%: You will submit weekly status update reports, both individually and as a team, and
hold a weekly status update meeting with your entire team and your instructor and TA during class
hours. Team members should take turns taking meeting minutes, submitting team reports, and leading
the weekly meeting discussion. Reports and meeting minutes should be
archived/backedup/copied/stored/committed/checked-in/etc. so that they are always and easily
accessible by the team and the instructors.

Recall Practice - 2%: Every class (including the first class) will start with a short quiz to practice recalling
information. They will include material about your assignments, questions about your design choices,
and knowledge that you should have gained from your past computer science courses. Research has
repeatedly shown that practicing recalling information, e.g. through quizzing, improves the ability to
recall information better than attending lectures or studying alone. Thus, this not only lets us know
where your knowledge might be lacking so that we may be able to correct it within this class, but it also
serves to help you acquire and access you the knowledge that you need to have.

Peer Feedback and Peer Evaluation - 3%: Peer feedback of others' work is crucial. It is important that
your feedback is of high quality. You will give feedback to your peers regularly throughout the semester
on their project ideas, written work, presentations, and demonstrations. In addition, each member of
your project team will evaluate the performance of your other team members at multiple points
throughout the semester. You will use these peer evaluations to adjust how you interact and collaborate
in your team. Failure to sufficiently participate in peer evaluations of your team members will result in
a letter grade reduction (where "sufficiently" means at least 70% and follows from the policy that any
component with a score less than 70% results in a letter grade reduction).

Ethics - 5%: You will complete various activities and assignments to explore the ethics in the discipline. All
team members must complete the CITI training before beginning a user study which is a required part of
the course.

Submission of Assignments:
All assignments will be turned in electronically though Canvas (unless otherwise specified) by the due
date and time given on the assignment. Email submissions will not be accepted. Unless otherwise noted
on the syllabus or in the assignment instructions, all assignments will be due by 8:00am on the due date
(before classes start for the day).

Submitting in-class assignments (such as recall practices, peer feedback, and assignments requiring
class interaction) while not physically attending class will be considered an academic integrity
violation, reported to the Aggie Honor System Office, and the penalty of F* in the course will be
assigned. This does not apply to weekly status update reports.

Industry-Style Performance Reviews:
Poorly performing students will be identified by the following metrics:
•
Quality of Work, e.g. low individual work scores, negative feedback from sponsor/TA/instructor
•
Initiative, e.g. refusing to take on responsibility
•
Teamwork, e.g. low peer evaluation scores, negative feedback on peer evaluations
•
Timely Delivery on Commitments, e.g. late submission of work, incomplete work
•
Effective Communication, e.g. becoming unresponsive, unprofessional communication
•
Sponsor/Client Focus, e.g. detracting from value to sponsor/client
Once a poorly performing student has been identified, they will be notified of their standing and made
aware of specific actions which can be taken to improve their performance. The student will be asked to
meet with the instructor and the TA. The agenda of the meeting will be:
1. Copy work record (meeting minutes, design diagrams, git commit logs, etc.)
2. Review of work record
3. Review of metrics
•
Quality of Work
•
Initiative
•
Teamwork
•
Timely Delivery on Commitments
•
Effective Communication
•
Sponsor/Client Focus
4. Improvement plan (SMART goals that must be met in order to pass the class)
5. Sign and date performance review (PR) document
6. Email copy of signed PR document to prof, TA, and student
The intention of this intervention is to support the student in making improving changes in their
participation and performance.

Purchases and Reimbursements:
•
Accurate Forms: Please ensure that purchase order (PO) forms submitted to the department are
accurate. Submitting a PO with incomplete specifications or back-ordered items increases the
workload for our accounting staff and causes unnecessary delays to your team. Submitting an
incomplete PO will result in the team being prevented from submitting additional POs; and all
additional purchases will have to be made by team members themselves and will be reimbursed
at the end of the semester.
•
In-Stock Items: Please verify that items are in stock before submitting a PO, and provide
alternative vendors if an item is likely to go out of stock (e.g., if the vendor only has a few units
left). If you suspect that a critical item may become back-ordered, it may be better if you
purchase it directly since POs may take several days to go through.
•
Reimbursements Deadline: Reimbursement requests are due at the time of final deliverables.
To minimize overhead on our accounting staff, we will only reimburse up to five purchase orders
(of your choice); this policy also encourages teams to plan their purchases in advance. Overnight
or express delivery will not be reimbursed unless approved in advance by the instructor.
Reimbursement of sales taxes is not allowed by the university; make sure to bring
taxexempt forms with you whenever you make local purchases (or purchases within the state).
•
Equipment Return: The equipment and materials that you purchase do not belong to you, they
belong to Texas A&M University. Before you will be given a final grade in the course, all
equipment and materials must be returned.

chunks.title: Class Participation
chunks.content:
The instructors will evaluate your attendance to class sessions, participation in the
discussions, and contributions to the team. More than 5 unexcused absences will result in a letter grade
reduction. Coming significantly late to class will count as an unexcused absence. (Excused absences do
not count toward this total.) Note that days in which teams may need to work in a remote location to
accomplish certain assignments (e.g., presenting your work at a conference, interviewing experts,
meeting with mentors, accessing technologies outside of the classroom, etc.) do not count toward
absences provided they are approved by the instructor in advance, abide by the instructor's
requirements for check-in during course time, abide by any activity reporting requirements by the
instructor, and all assignments relevant to that class time are completed on-time.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Assignments will be considered late after their posted deadline but will be accepted until the closure
date of the assignment. (Note that all recall practices and some assignments must be completed within a
class session and will not be accepted late. In addition, some assignments such as status reports, peer
evaluations, proposals, etc. are specific to a particular phase of the course or project development and
may have shorter windows where they will be accepted.) Late assignments will receive a grade reduction
of 10% per day late.

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is
exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Below is the planned but tentative schedule of topics and major projects for the course. All assignment
and project details will be communicated on Canvas.

| Weeks | Course Phases | Major Deliverables |
|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | Introduction, Team Formation, and Project Exploration You will learn how to identify and formulate an engineering problem and how to investigate problem needs to better inform your proposed solution. You will also explore elements of good and bad design. You will practice communicating your problem's motivation and user needs. | Project proposal (due by end of Week 2) |
| 3 | Project Ideation and Design You will learn how to use design thinking to create innovative solutions to your problem and how to define project success. You will develop user stories and usage scenarios and learn how to effectively prototype and test different interface designs. You will participate in rapid user studies with peers on initial lo-fi prototypes, identify design moves in response, and create a final system design. You will begin to make iterative progress on your final written report. | Incremental submission of final report elements (various, see Canvas assignments) |
| 4-11 | Project Development You will complete a literature review tailored for your project that places your work in the context of existing work and practice communicating your solution and design. You will also create a project management plan based on software development methodologies, begin participating in peer evaluations within your team, and complete ethics training. You will implement your system design following standard software development methodologies and using GitHub for version control. You will submit a new code release and demo your project to the class every 2 weeks. You will also submit weekly status updates and hold weekly update meetings. At the end of this phase, you will have a full working system implemented and be ready for system evaluation. You will also continue to make iterative progress on your final written report. | Finalized development schedule (due by end of Week 3)
Incremental submission of final report elements (various, see Canvas assignments)
Fully working system (due by end of Week 11) |
| 12-13 | Project Evaluation You will thoroughly test your system's functionality according to your evaluation plan. You will also conduct a user study and analyze collected data to evaluate the success of your system. You will communicate results from these two types of evaluations in your final report. You will continue to submit weekly status updates, hold weekly update meetings, demo your project to the class, and participate in peer evaluations. | Fully evaluated system (due by end of Week 13) |
| 14 | Project Finalization You will complete your final report and finish preparing all final deliverables. You will practice communicating your problem, solution, and results to the class. You will submit your last weekly status update and participate in your last peer evaluation. | Complete draft of final deliverables (due by end of Week 14) |
| 15 | Project Presentations You will present your final projects to industry panelists and participate in exit interviews. | All final deliverables (due on last day of class by 11:59pm)
Final Presentations to IAP members and exit interviews. |

Traditionally Delivered Course – This is a 15 week, traditionally delivered Face-to-face course.

chunks.title: Inclusion Statement
chunks.content:
In this class we are committed to a culture of inclusivity. We reject and condemn all forms of injustice
and actively promote diversity and inclusion within our field. As Martin Luther King Jr. said, “Injustice
anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere." If any student wishes to discuss anything regarding
inclusivity within the classroom, especially anything contrary to this statement that you have
experienced, you are encouraged to contact the instructor or TA.

chunks.title: Personal Accessibility Statement
chunks.content:
I recognize disability as an aspect of diversity, equity, and inclusion. For that reason, I try to design all of
my course material to be inclusive and accessible. However, I realize that some aspects of the course
may not yet be as inclusive as they could be. I rely in part on my students to let me know when and
where they need some accommodation. I want you to know that my top priority is for my students to
learn as much as they can in my class. That includes working with students to find solutions that help
them perform at their best. I hope tools like the Disability Accommodation Portal will make it easier for
students to start the conversation about how we can work together to help them make the most out of
their learning opportunities.

chunks.title: Course Copyright
chunks.content:
The materials used within this course are copyrighted. These materials include, but are not limited to,
the syllabus, quizzes, assignments, activity descriptions, online content, course videos, audio and visual
recordings of classes, etc. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy or
distribute these materials, unless permission is expressly granted.

chunks.title: Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility. Students
are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused absences, including
definitions, and related documentation and timelines.
•
Importance of Attendance: Not attending class sessions harms the other members of your team
and makes it much more difficult for the instructor to assess your contributions to the team
effort. Therefore, attendance, punctuality, and active participation in class sessions are required.
Due to the unfortunate consequences and undue burden your frequent absences would have on
the rest of the team, more than 5 unexcused absences will result in a letter grade reduction for
the course. (Excused absences do not count toward this total.)
•
Absence and Tardiness: Failure to attend a class session or late arrivals (more than 25 minutes
late) will be counted as unexcused absences. Lateness or absences can be excused if there is a
valid reason. Illness, job interviews out of town, death in the family, and conference attendance
are examples of valid reasons. To request an absence be considered as excused, submit
documentation to the Excused Absence Documentation assignment on Canvas. Documentation
will not be accepted over email.
•
Valid Excuses: Oversleeping, a term paper due, an exam to cram for, etc., are not valid reasons.
The instructor reserves the right to determine what constitutes a valid reason on a case-by-case
basis.
•
Proper Planning: If you know you are going to be late or miss a class, please let the instructor
and your teammates know, so that they may plan for your absence and make the best use of
their time.

chunks.title: Communication Policy
chunks.content:
We will use Canvas to post assignments, course resources, discussions, send and receive emails, and
send announcements. It is your responsibility to check Canvas and pay attention to class emails and
Canvas announcements.

chunks.title: Bring Your Own Device Policy
chunks.content:
BYOD is an initiative in the College of Engineering where students are required to bring their own
computing device to lecture and lab. You need to have a computing device with a headset, microphone,
and webcam. You will also need stable internet access outside of lecture and lab. See the BYOD
webpage for information on the program, approved devices, and financial assistance. BYOD devices
fulfill the requirements for this class.

chunks.title: Generative AI Policy
chunks.content:
This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students themselves.
Students should not have another person or entity do the creation, writing, or revision of any
substantive portion of an assignment for them (including software/code) unless instructed to do so by
the instructor, which includes hiring a person or company or using artificial intelligence tools like
ChatGPT or CoPilot to create, write, or revise assignments. Software projects may use generative
artificial intelligence tools within them (similar to how other software libraries, APIs, and packages are
used) as long as students receive prior instructor approval and properly document its usage, but
generative artificial intelligence tools may not be used to create the software project/code itself.
(adapted from University of Massachusetts Amherst, https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/1d5e4ef6-
97f14065-987f-3c9dfecbb7bd/TAMU-CTE GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsiderations.pdf)
Any violation of this policy will be considered an academic integrity violation, reported to the Aggie
Honor System Office, and the penalty of F* in the course will be assigned.

chunks.title: Texas A&M at College Station
chunks.content:
Students wishing to discuss concerns related to mental and/or physical health in a confidential setting
are encouraged to make an appointment with University Health Services or download the TELUS Health
Student Support app for 24/7 access to professional counseling in multiple languages. Walk-in services
for urgent, non-emergency needs are available during normal business hours at University Health
Services locations; call 979.458.4584 for details.

Students can learn more about filing a report, accessing supportive resources, and navigating the Title IX
investigation and resolution process on the University's Title IX webpage.

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical factors influencing a
student's academic success and overall wellbeing. Students are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care
practices by utilizing the resources and services available through University Health Services. Students
needing a listening ear can call the Texas A&M Helpline (979.845.2700) from 4:00 p.m. to 8:00 a.m.
weekdays and 24 hours on weekends for mental health peer support while classes are in session. The
TELUS Health Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple languages
anytime, anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency
support at 988 or 988lifeline.org.

Texas A&M College Station
Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services (979.458.4584) or call the Texas
A&M Helpline (979.845.2700) from 4:00p.m. to 8:00 a.m. weekdays and 24 hours on weekends while
classes are in session. 24-hour emergency help is also available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline
(988) or at 988lifeline.org.

chunks.title: Artificial Intelligence Statement
chunks.content:
With the emergence of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, the ways in which we define our
creative processes continue to transform. Al generators are rapidly evolving from simple editing
for grammatical errors and spelling mistakes (Grammarly, MS Word Spell Check) to sophisticated
text production (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.), as well as image, computer code, and audio
generation. The presence of such tools, however, does not replace our need to learn how to
draft, revise, and reflect on texts, programs, drawings and how to exercise information literacy
and personal responsibility in how we locate, evaluate, incorporate, and cite primary/ secondary
sources. For example, the Association for Writing Across the Curriculum states the
following: Writing to learn is an intellectual activity that is crucial to the cognitive and social
development of learners and writers. This vital activity cannot be replaced by Al language
generators (AWAC).

Engaging in the various aspects of creative pursuits (e.g., writing, coding, drawing) is critical
to education in a broad sense. While Al technologies will continue shaping how we
approach these creative tasks, the critical work of creativity relies on integrity, originality,
and ethical conduct in regard to appropriate representation as an author or creator. Thus,
submitting work with a significant percentage of Al-generated content, unless otherwise
permitted, can be considered academic misconduct under Texas A&M University Student
Rule 20. Students must therefore cite the use of Generative Al tools and document what
they have contributed to an assignment.

warnings: Schedule has no specific dates, only week numbers and 'end of Week X' deadlines.
